segments.s556-g113: جهاندار is a lofty title 'Lord of the world / sovereign' (subject). برزد is an archaic past verb here meaning 'sent / came' (lit. 'struck/occurred'); یکی before باد is the indefinite 'a / a certain'. The phrase یکی باد سرد = 'a cold wind' is the theme introduced by the verb (poetic word order, copula/auxiliary omitted).
segments.s556-g114: پس 'then' links the consequence. آن لعل رخسارگان is a genitive/nominal phrase 'that ruby of the cheeks' (لعل = ruby; رخسارگان = cheeks/faces). کرد زرد is a causative/disciplinary construction: کرد here means 'made/caused to be' and زرد 'pale/wan' describes the resulting state.
segments.s556-g115: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when / as'. رنگین رخ تاجور is a noun phrase with adjective before noun: 'the colorful-faced merchant' (رنگین modifies رخ, and تاجور identifies the person). تیره شد is the predicate 'became dark / grew gloomy' with شد as the past auxiliary 'became'.
segments.s556-g116: ازان 'from/thereupon' introduces the cause/result phrase. درد here is emotional 'sorrow / pain' (not merely physical). بهرام دل is a poetic compact order meaning 'Bahram's heart' (possessive relation expressed without ezâfe for metrical reasons), and خیره شد = 'became stunned/astonished' (شد = became).
segments.s556-g117: چهل روز is a temporal phrase 'for forty days'. بد here means 'ill / in bad condition' and modifies the following nominal descriptors. سوکوار 'archer' and نژند 'base / dishonoured' are predicative/characterizing terms; the copula (was/lay) is omitted as is common in poetry.
segments.s556-g118: پر از گرد is the idiom 'full of dust' (پر = full (of); از = of; گرد = dust). و بیکار links another predicate 'and unused/idle' (بیکار describing neglect). تخت بلند 'the high throne' appears at the end as the thing described; the copula 'was' is again omitted.
segments.s556-g119: چنین بود is a fixed phrase 'thus it was / so it was'. تا here functions as 'as long as / while', and گردان سپهر is a poetic epithet 'the revolving/turning heavens' (گردان qualifying سپهر). The repetition of بود is rhythmic/poetic ('it was... as long as it was...').
segments.s556-g120: گهی ... و گهی ... is an alternating adverbial pair 'sometimes ... and sometimes ...'. پر ز is 'full of' (پر + ز), followed first by درد 'sorrow/affliction' and then مهر 'love/affection', describing the alternating states (of the heavens or fate).
segments.s556-g17: روانت is روان + enclitic -ت (your), ‘your spirit/soul’. خرد باد is a predicative phrase where باد is the optative particle meaning “may it be”; thus خرد باد = “may (it be) wisdom/wise.” دستور شرم is a noun phrase ‘dastur (rule/order) of sharm (modesty)’, with the ezāfe omitted as is common in poetry; the initial و links the two wished-for qualities and the optative باد scopes over them.
segments.s556-g18: سخن گفتن is a gerund/infinitival noun phrase ‘speaking’ (سخن = speech, گفتن = to say/speak). خوب here qualifies the manner (‘well’). The conjunction و links this phrase to آواز نرم, where آواز نرم is a noun + adjective ‘a gentle/soft voice’; both halves describe desirable manners of expression.
segments.s556-g19: خداوند پیروز is a titled noun phrase ‘the victorious Lord/God’ (epithet follows the noun). یار تو باد has the possessive clitic -ت on یار and باد as the optative particle ‘may (he) be’, so the whole means ‘may the victorious Lord be your companion/ally’. Word order places the epithet before the optative predicate in typical poetic style.
segments.s556-g20: دل زیردستان is a genitive relationship ‘the hearts of (thy) subordinates/subjects’ (the ezāfe is omitted in poetry). شکار تو باد repeats the optative construction: شکار = ‘prey/conquest’ is the predicate and باد = ‘may it be’, so ‘may the hearts of your subjects be your conquest’ (i.e. you win their hearts).
segments.s556-g21: بنه here is an imperative ‘put/lay (aside)’ (an archaic/poetic imperative form), followed by کینه ‘hatred/grudge’ as its object. و connects the second imperative clause دور باش (‘be far’), where دور is an adjectival/adverbial idea ‘far, distant’ and باش is the imperative ‘be’. از هوا uses از + هوا (‘from desire’) to indicate separation: دور باش از هوا = ‘keep far from desire’.
segments.s556-g22: مبادا introduces a negative purposive/warnive clause ‘lest/may it not be that…’. هوا is the subject (‘desire/passion’), بر تو uses the preposition بر ‘over/on’ with the second-person clitic تو, and فرمانروا is a predicate noun ‘ruler/sovereign’. The clause therefore reads ‘lest desire be ruler over you.’
segments.s556-g23: This is a simple coordinated list: سخن‌چین ‘tattletale/informer’, و links, بی‌دانش ‘the ignorant/unlearned’, و links again, چاره‌گر ‘one who claims to find a remedy/solver’ (here pejorative: a would‑be fixer). All three are objects or topics picked up by the following negative clause; the conjunctions simply enumerate the undesirable types.
segments.s556-g24: نباید is an impersonal prohibition ‘it must not / one should not’; که is the subordinating particle ‘that’. یابد is the third-person present/subjunctive of یاب (to find/obtain) here meaning ‘find/obtain’ (with an injunctive sense driven by نباید). به پیشت گذر is a prepositional phrase ‘a passage into/ before you’ (پیشت = ‘in your presence/before you’), so the whole clause means ‘they should not find passage before you.’
segments.s556-g105: بخش is an imperative verb here ('give!') taking a dative introduced by به: 'به + recipient + بخش' = 'give to X'. هرچت is the contracted/poetic form of هر چه تو ('whatever you'), and هواست is a two-part predicate (هوا + است) meaning 'is (your) wish' or 'is your whim'; the clause literally reads 'give to the arzanians whatever is your desire.' Note the archaic contraction هرچت and the poetic placement of the copula as -ست.
segments.s556-g106: که introduces the explanatory/casual clause ('for' / 'that'). گنج تو is the noun + possessive pattern ('your treasure'), with تو postposed as is common in poetry. ارزانیان را سزاست is a predicative construction: سزاست ('it is deserved / fitting') takes ارزانیان را as the beneficiary/experiencer marked by را. So the clause says 'for your treasure is fitting/deserved for the arzanians.'
segments.s556-g107: بکش is an imperative (here meaning 'pull out', 'give up' or 'lay down' — i.e., 'sacrifice/expel'); it governs the coordinated objects جان و دل ('life and heart'). تا توانی is a common idiom meaning 'as much as you can' (تا + present/subjunctive توانی); ز is the poetic form of از meaning 'because of' or 'out of', followed by رشک 'envy/jealousy' as the motivating cause.
segments.s556-g108: که introduces the resulting/descriptive clause: 'that envy brings ...'. آورد is a classical third‑person verb ('brings/causes' or 'arouses') used here in a general/present sense. گرم و خونین form a coordinated adjective phrase modifying سرشک ('a hot and blood‑stained teardrop'); adjective + noun order is standard, and سرشک is a poetic word for 'tear'.
segments.s556-g109: هرانگه که is a subordinating temporal clause meaning 'whenever' / 'when'. رشک آورد پادشا is an archaic/poetic ordering meaning 'envy came upon the king' (i.e. 'the king was seized by envy'); آورد here expresses 'comes/strikes' and the experiencer 'پادشا' follows in the poetic inversion typical of Ferdowsi.
segments.s556-g110: نکوهش کند is a verbal phrase meaning 'make reproach / reproach (him)' where کند is the auxiliary/verb 'do' (habitual or modal). مردم پارسا is the agent/subject 'the pious people'; note the verb-before-subject word order (inversion) used for metrical/poetic effect: literally 'reproach do the pious people'.
segments.s556-g111: چو = 'when'. اندرز بنوشت contains بنوشت, an archaic/past tense form meaning 'wrote' (بنوشــت/نوشت), so the clause reads 'when [he] wrote the counsel'. فرخ دبیر is a name + title ('Farrokh the secretary/scribe') placed after the verb by poetic inversion: agent follows the verb here.
segments.s556-g112: بیاورد و بنهاد are two coordinated past actions ('he brought' and 'he placed/put'); the conjunction و links them. پیش here means 'before / in the presence of' and وزیر is the indirect object/target ('the vizier'). The order is Verb (brought) + and + Verb (placed) + prepositional phrase 'پیش وزیر'.
segments.s556-g1: چو دانست introduces a temporal subordinate clause: چو = 'when' + دانست = past 'he knew/realized'. کز begins a complement clause 'that ...' and is followed by مرگ نتوان گریخت: نتوان (negative modal 'cannot') + گریخت (the verbal root/past form 'escape') together mean 'cannot escape'. Word order is typical for older/poetic Persian: temporal marker + reporting verb + complement clause.
segments.s556-g2: بسی is an adverb meaning 'much/a great deal'; آب خونین is a noun phrase 'bloody water' (metaphorical for bloody tears); ز دیده is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from the eye(s)' using ز = 'from'; بریخت is a past-tense verb 'he poured/shed'. The verb comes at the end as usual and the clause describes an accomplished action following the realization.
segments.s556-g3: بگسترد is a past 3rd-person verb 'he spread/laid out'. فرش is the direct object 'a carpet/ rug'. اندر ایوان خویش is a locative phrase 'in his iwan/porch' (اندر = 'in', ایوان = 'iwan', خویش + ش = reflexive possessive 'his own'). The verb + object + locative order is typical in narrative/poetic register.
segments.s556-g4: بفرمود is a formal/honorific past verb meaning 'he said/ordered'. کامدش is an archaic verbal form meaning 'let him come / he come' with the enclitic -ش referring to 'him' (object). بهرام is the addressee's name and پیش indicates direction 'before/in the presence of' (come forward). The structure is verb (ordered) + quoted/commanded action.
segments.s556-g5: بدو is a prepositional pronoun 'to him/unto him'. گفت is the past verb 'said' (here used as a reporting verb). کای is a vocative particle 'O' addressing the addressee; پاک‌زاده پسر is a vocative nominal phrase 'noble-born son' with adjective before noun, a standard attributive order in Persian.
segments.s556-g6: به marks the domain 'in/regarding'; مردی here means 'manliness/valor' (abstract noun), و = 'and', دانش = 'knowledge/learning'. برآورده is a past participle/adjective 'raised/attained' used predicatively with سر 'head' (literally 'you have lifted your head in manliness and learning' i.e. 'you have excelled'). The participle phrase attributes the achievement to the addressed person.
segments.s556-g7: به من marks the beneficiary/target 'to me'; پادشاهی = 'kingship/kingshiphood' is the subject or theme; نهادست is a stative/perfective verbal form meaning 'has been placed' or 'is set' (poetic/archaic), and روی (here 'appearance/face') qualifies the statement—together implying that kingship has been conferred or is innate in his appearance. The clause uses poetic inversion and a stative verb.
segments.s556-g8: که introduces a causal/explanatory clause 'for/because': رنگ = 'color/complexion', رخم here is the poetically attached form meaning 'my cheek/face' (رخ + م = 'my face') and functions as the possessor phrase 'the color of my face'; کرد = 'made/caused', همرنگ = 'the same color', موی = 'hair'. The clause explains why kingship/appearance is as stated: his complexion makes his face the color of his hair. Note some forms are contracted/poetic (رخم = 'my face').
segments.s556-g57: 'به دل' is a prepositional phrase 'into/for the heart' (preposition + noun). 'نیز' is an adverb 'also/too' giving emphasis. 'اندیشه بد' is a noun phrase 'evil thought'. 'مدار' here is the noun 'centre/sphere' (literary/poetic) — the clause says literally 'make evil thought the sphere/centre of the heart', with the negative sense carried from the preceding context (an admonition not to do so).
segments.s556-g58: 'بداندیش را' uses the object/ topicalizing marker 'را' to mark 'the ill‑minded' as the affected party ('to/for the ill‑minded'). Word order is inverted for poetic effect: 'بد بود روزگار' = 'the times were bad.' 'بد' is predicate adjective 'bad/unfavourable' and 'بود' is the past copula 'was' — so literally, 'for the ill‑minded, the times were bad.'
segments.s556-g59: Word order: 'سپهبد' (the spahbed) is the topic/subject, 'کجا' is the question adverb 'where', and 'گشت' is the past verb 'became/turned (into)'. 'پیمان‑شکن' is the predicate 'oath‑breaker/faithless one' — so 'Where has the spahbed become an oath‑breaker?'.
segments.s556-g60: 'بخندد' is a third‑person verb form often used for a general or potential action ('would/should laugh' or 'may laugh'); the verb comes first (poetic inversion). 'بدو' = 'at him/to him' (dative). 'نامدار انجمن' is a noun phrase 'the famed/renowned assembly' (subject). Together: 'Would the famed assembly laugh at him?'.
segments.s556-g61: 'خردگیر' is a vocative/noun 'wise/prudent person' (address). 'ک' is the clitic contraction of 'که' introducing a relative clause 'whose'. 'آرایش جان' = 'the ornament/adornment of the soul' (genitive relationship). The final 'تست' in classical Persian here is an archaic/copular form meaning 'thou art'/'is thine' (not covered by the provided senses) — the phrase as a whole: 'O wise one, whose ornament is the soul...'.
segments.s556-g62: 'نگهدار' is an imperative 'keep/preserve' (here a verb addressing the addressee). 'گفتار و پیمان' is a coordinated direct object 'speech/word and covenant/oath.' The final 'تست' again is the archaic/copular/possessive form ('are yours' / 'thine') in context; the practical reading is imperative: 'Preserve your speech and your covenant.' (the provided senses do not list this copular use, so it is marked unavailable.)
segments.s556-g63: هم at the start is an adverb meaning “even/also,” modifying the whole noun phrase that follows. آرایش is the head noun (“array/display/ornament”) and takes the series تاج و گنج و سپاه as its objects/contents; the three nouns are simply linked by و (‘and’), with the ezāfe (ـِ) commonly omitted in epic poetry when listing items.
segments.s556-g64: نماینده is a predicative noun meaning “representative/manifestation” and stands for an implied copula (i.e. “is representative of” — the verb ‘to be’ is omitted as is normal in Persian poetry). گردش هور و ماه is a genitive-like complement: گردش (“turning/circuit/rotation”) takes هور و ماه (‘sun and moon’) as its domain; هور is an archaic/poetic form referring to solar light/sun and ماه is the moon.
segments.s556-g97: شود is the present subjunctive/optative of شدن meaning "become" (here a wish). جانت is a possessive compound (جان + -ت) meaning "your life/soul." از normally means "from/than" in comparative constructions here linking جانت to the standard of comparison (دشمن). آژیرتر is a comparative adjective (تر suffix) built on آژیر ("alarm/siren"), so literally "more alarm‑like/keener"; the phrase means "become keener/alert than the enemy."
segments.s556-g98: This is a triad of coordinated items (دل و مغز و رایت) followed by the comparative adjective جهانگیرتر. The conjunction و simply links the nouns; جهانگیرتر is the comparative form of جهانگیر ("world‑conquering") and modifies all three items (heart, brain/mind, and banner/standard). The order is noun-noun-adjective (adjective after the list) as is common in classical Persian.
segments.s556-g99: کسی را is fronted with the object/topic marker را (here "anyone" as a topical/indefinite referent). The core predicate is کجا پیش رو شد هوا — literally "where (کجا) before/in front (پیش) an appearance/face (رو) became (شد) desire (هوا)." In idiomatic English: "where desire appeared before anyone." The verb شد is the past/become verb marking the event.
segments.s556-g100: چنان دان is an imperative/hortative use of دانستن meaning "consider/think thus." The particle که introduces the following result/clause. رایش is the possessive + noun "his judgement/opinion," and نگیرد is a negative present subjunctive/optative of گرفتن (here "may not seize/take"); نوا ("melody/sound") is the direct object of نگیرد. So: "Consider it so that his judgment does not seize the melody."
segments.s556-g101: اگر introduces a conditional clause. دوست یابد is a present/future-oriented verb phrase (یابد = "finds/comes upon"); the following ت (the 2nd‑person suffix) plus را mark the addressee as the object ("finds you"). تازه‌روی is a descriptive noun/adjective ("fresh-faced/newcomer") serving as predicate complementation: "If a friend finds you fresh‑faced..."
segments.s556-g102: بیفزاید is an optative/imperative form meaning "add/increase" (let it add). این نام را places "this name/reputation" as the definite object (را). رنگ و بوی are coordinated nouns "colour and scent," the things to be added to the name. Word order is verb + object + complements (typical older Persian).
segments.s556-g103: تو is the subject "you." با دشمنت is a prepositional phrase "with your enemy." رو here means "face/appearance," and پر + noun is a compound meaning "full of X" (پر آژنگ = "full of clang/noise"). دار at the end functions as an imperative/hortative "keep/have" (classical usage), so the clause instructs: "You, with your enemy, keep your face full of clatter/noise."
segments.s556-g104: بداندیش را places the ill‑minded person as a topical/indirect object ("to the malcontent"). چهره بی‌رنگ دار repeats the imperative/possessive sense of دار with a descriptive phrase "a colourless face/appearance" (بی‌رنگ = "without colour/colourless"). The clause means: "Give/keep for the ill‑minded a colourless visage."
segments.s556-g89: Imperative clause: object phrases 'زبان و خرد' (‘speech/tongue and wisdom’) are combined by و and governed by the imperative predicate 'راست کن' (‘make straight/right’). 'با دلت' uses the preposition با + the possessive suffix ت on دل (دلت) to mean 'with your heart' or 'in accord with your heart'. 'راست کن' is ساختِ فعل مرکب: adjective/sta te 'راست' + the causative/doer verb 'کن' giving 'make right/align'.
segments.s556-g90: 'همی' here is an emphatic/adverbial particle attached to the following verb; 'ران' is an imperative (archaic) meaning 'drive/urge' (from راندن) and therefore directs the action toward the previously mentioned 'زبان و خرد'. 'ازان سان که' is a circumstantial/relative phrase 'in that manner which…'; 'خواهی' is 2nd-person present/subjunctive meaning 'you wish' and introduces the content of the relative clause; 'سخن' is the object 'speech/words'.
segments.s556-g91: Relative clause with the indefinite subject 'هرانکس که' ('whoever that'): 'اندر سرش' = 'in his head' (location) where ش is the 3rd-person possessive suffix; 'مغز بود' places the predicate at the end in past tense ('had brain' / 'there was brain in his head'). The clause means 'whoever has intelligence in his head'.
segments.s556-g92: Main clause: 'همه رای و گفتار او' (‘all his counsel and speech’) is the subject phrase; the predicate 'نغز بود' is copular past 'was/was found' with adjective 'نغز' meaning apt/witty/pithy. Word order places possessor 'او' after the coordinated subject nouns, a common Classical Persian pattern.
segments.s556-g93: 'هرانگه که' = 'whenever/at such times that'; 'باشی' is a subjunctive/present form of بودن used in the subordinate clause meaning 'you are' (archaic form, not an imperative here); 'تو' is the 2nd-person pronoun; 'با رای‌زن' means 'with a counselor' (با + noun). The clause sets the condition/time for the following advice.
segments.s556-g94: Predicate/advice: 'سخنها' (plural 'words/speeches') is described by 'بیارای' — an adjective/description meaning 'unadorned/plain' (i.e., do not embellish). 'بی‌انجمن' is an adverbial/qualifying phrase 'without assembly/company' (meaning in private council or without flattering a crowd). Together they advise: in council, keep your words plain and not embellished for show.
segments.s556-g95: 'گرت' is the archaic conditional 'if you' (contraction of اگر + ت). The clause 'رای با آزمایش بود' states the condition: 'رای' = counsel/opinion, 'با آزمایش' = 'with test/experience' (i.e., tested/proven by trial), and 'بود' is the copula in past/conditional sense. Overall: 'If your counsel is backed by experience…'.
segments.s556-g96: Result clause: 'همه روزت' = 'all your days' (همه + روز + possessive suffix ت), 'اندر فزایش بود' places the predicate at the end with اندر = 'in', 'فزایش' = 'increase/prosperity', and بود = 'would be / will be'. The line promises continual growth or prosperity as the outcome of experienced counsel.
segments.s556-g81: فزونیش is a nominalized/possessive form meaning 'its increase/abundance' (the -ش = its). هر روز 'every day' precedes the verb phrase as a time adverbial. افزون شود is a present/subjunctive-style verbal phrase 'becomes greater' (افزون = 'increasing/more'; شود = 'becomes/is'), so the clause literally says 'its increase becomes more every day.'
segments.s556-g82: شتاب آورد is a light verbal phrase meaning 'haste came / one became hasty' (شتاب as noun 'haste', آورد used causatively/lexically 'brought/caused' → 'was seized by haste'). دل پر از خون شود is a coordinated clause: دل (heart) marked by no additional case marker, پر از خون 'full of blood' (پر = 'full (of)', از = 'of/from', خون = 'blood'), with شود again 'becomes/iscome to be'.
segments.s556-g83: هرانکس که introduces a free relative 'whoever/whosoever'. با آب دریا is an instrumental/with-phrase 'with (the) sea's water' (با = 'with', آب = 'water', دریا = 'sea'). نبرد here is the notion of 'fighting/contending' (classical poetic use where the noun/verb sense overlaps): the whole relative clause reads 'whoever contends against the sea with water.'
segments.s556-g84: بجوید is a present-tense/formal-subjunctive verb meaning 'seeks/attempts' (here the action of seeking/attempting the contest). نباشد is a literary negative copula meaning 'is not' (third-person negative; an older subjunctive/optative form of بودن). خردمند مرد is the predicative nominal phrase 'a wise man' or more idiomatically 'no wise man' when negated; word order is object/predicate after the verb in poetic style.
segments.s556-g85: This is an imperative-like image: کمان ... دار has the sense 'let/be (a) bow' or 'make ... a bow' (کمان = 'bow', دار here functions as a predicate/possessive/imperative element in older poetic usage). دل را uses the object marker را to mark 'the heart' as the thing to be treated as a bow. زبانت چو تیر is a simile 'your tongue like an arrow' (چو = 'like/as', تیر = 'arrow'), so the clause pairs heart↔bow and tongue↔arrow.
segments.s556-g86: تو is the emphatic vocative 'you'. این گفته‌های من 'these my sayings' (این = 'these/this', گفته‌های = plural 'sayings/statements', من = 'my'). آسان مگیر is an imperative negative 'do not take [them] lightly/easily' (آسان here adverbial 'easily', مگیر = negative imperative 'do not take').
segments.s556-g87: گشاد پرت باشد is a conditional/optative-styled clause 'if/when the shot is wide' (گشاد = 'wide', پرت = 'shot/projectile' or 'that which is thrown', باشد = 'is/let it be'). و links to the following noun phrase دست راست 'right hand' (دست = 'hand', راست = 'right'). The clause sets up a situation about the arrow/shot and the right hand as target or reference.
segments.s556-g88: نشانه بنه is an older/poetic imperative construction meaning 'place/set a mark' (نشانه = 'sign/mark', بنه here is archaic poetic 'put/place'). زان نشان 'from/that mark' (زان = 'from that/therefore', نشان = 'mark/sign') is followed by کت هواست: ست is the copula 'is', and هواست = 'is (of) desire/whim' (هوا = 'desire/passion'); the intervening کت is an obscure/poetic element here (likely dialectal or contracted) and functions inside the phrase. Overall: 'set a mark on the right hand; that mark is a sign of caprice/desire.'
segments.s556-g73: Word order places the object phrase شکست تو (your defeat) before the verb جوید (seeks), a common poetic order. همی is the archaic/progressive adverb meaning 'continually/always' modifying جوید. زان سخن ('from that speech') names the source; zā(n) + noun = 'from that/that (aforementioned) speech.'
segments.s556-g74: ممان here is an older negative-imperative/jussive sense 'do not withhold/oppose' (addressing 'you'). تا introduces a temporal/subordinating clause 'until'; the subordinate verb گردد (گردیدن used here) is in the present/subjunctive sense 'becomes'. به پیش تو = 'before/in the presence of you' (به + پیش), and کهن means 'old'. Together: 'do not oppose until it becomes old before you.'
segments.s556-g75: کسی + کش here produces a compound sense 'a person who draws/earns (something)' — i.e., 'a praise-drawing person' or 'one whose praise is gained.' ستایش بیاید به کار literally 'praise may come to use' = 'praise proves useful/comes to your service'; بیاید is an optative/possible-present 'may come / comes.' به کار = 'to (your) affairs / to use.'
segments.s556-g76: This is a direct address to 'تو' (you). او را is the object/target 'him.' ز گیتی 'from the world' and به مردم 'to the people' set up two spheres; مدار is used idiomatically here as 'do not place/assign (him) in the realm/sphere of the common people' — i.e., 'do not treat him as merely one of the worldly crowd.' (Note: مدار is a noun 'sphere/realm' used idiomatically to indicate belonging/placement.)
segments.s556-g77: که introduces a causal clause 'for / because.' یزدان is 'God'; ستایش is 'praise.' نخواهد is the older/fixed negative-future/present 'does not want / will not accept' (here 'does not desire'), and همی is the emphatic/progressive adverb 'indeed/always.'
segments.s556-g78: نکوهیده را is the topicalized object 'the one who is blamed/censured' (نکوهیده = 'censured'). دل is subject 'the heart.' بکاهد (from کاستن) here means 'will lessen / abase' (i.e., diminish courage/cheer); همی again marks continued action. Word order (object — subject — verb) is common in classical Persian poetry.
segments.s556-g79: هرانکس که = 'whoever; whosoever.' او is the actor 'he.' از گنهکار چشم بخوابید is an idiom: 'چشم بخوابیدن (برِ کسی)' = 'to close one's eye to someone / to avert one's gaze from a sinner' — literally 'to make the eye sleep from the sinner.' از marks the target (the sinner).
segments.s556-g80: بخوابید here continues the idiom (the verb 'to close/sleep' the eye). و links clauses. آسان is an adverb 'easily/gently.' فرو + برد is a light-verb construction meaning 'to put down / to swallow/repress' (فرو = downwards/intensive, برد = carry/take) so فرو برد خشم = 'repress/put away anger.' The overall sense: such a person easily refrains from anger.
segments.s556-g25: ز is the preposition 'from' and introduces the source. نادان is the noun 'the ignorant' (topic/source). نیابی is the verb formed with the present/subjunctive/imperative morphological shape here meaning 'you do not/find not' (classical usage often reads as 'you will not find' or 'you do not find'). جز از بتری is an exception phrase following the verb: جز 'except' + از 'from' + بتری 'worse' — together 'nothing except worse.'
segments.s556-g26: نگر is an exhortative imperative 'look/consider' (here functioning like 'take care' or 'note'), سوی is the preposition 'toward', بی‌دانشان refers to 'the unlearned/ignorant (people)', and ننگری is the verb form with the negative-prefix shape (here 'do not look' / 'you should not gaze') — so the clause commands: 'do not look toward the unlearned.'
segments.s556-g27: چنان دان is an imperative phrase: چنان 'so/such' + دان (imperative of 'to know/consider') = 'consider thus' or 'think of (him) in such a way.' که is the subordinating conjunction 'that' introducing the following description. بی‌شرم و بسیارگوی are two coordinated adjectives/nouns 'shameless and much‑speaking (talkative)'.
segments.s556-g28: نبیند is a third‑person negative present/presumptive form ('does not see' or idiomatically 'has not/does not possess'); the phrase به نزد کسی is a prepositional phrase 'in the presence of/ before anyone' (به + نزد + کسی), and آب‌روی ('reputation, honor') is the direct object/semantic head. Together: 'they have no reputation before anyone.'
segments.s556-g29: This clause is elliptical/imperative in sense. خرد را مه literally 'wisdom (marked by را for topical emphasis) [be] moon' — a metaphor 'let wisdom be your guiding moon.' و links two coordinated commands; خشم را بنده‌دار uses را again to topicalize 'anger' and بنده‌دار functions as 'keep as a servant/keep under control' (an instruction to master one’s anger).
segments.s556-g30: مشو is the negative imperative 'do not become/ do not be.' تیز is an adjective 'sharp/harsh/quick' describing the state not to assume. با introduces the person affected 'with', مرد is 'man/person', and پرهیزگار is the adjective 'pious/self‑restrained.' Together: 'Do not be harsh with a pious man.'
segments.s556-g31: نگر is an imperative 'take care/consider' (advice). تا introduces a purposive/negative‑warning clause here ('so that/lest'), and نگردد is the negative form of 'become/turn' ( ن‑ + گردد ) meaning 'does not come/does not turn' — followed by the locative phrase به گرد تو 'around/about you' and the noun آز 'greed.' Overall: 'Be careful lest greed come around you.'
segments.s556-g32: که is a connective introducing the reason/explanation 'for/that.' آز is the subject 'greed.' آورد is the verb 'brings/causes' (third person), and خشم و بیم و نیاز are the coordinated effects: 'anger and fear and need.' The clause explains why one should beware: 'for greed brings anger, fear, and want.'
segments.s556-g49: نگه‌دار here functions as an imperative 'keep/guard' (classical/poetic register). تا introduces a purpose/result clause 'so that', and مردم عیب‌جوی is a noun phrase where عیب‌جوی modifies مردم ('fault‑finding people').
segments.s556-g50: نجوید is a negative 3rd‑person present/subjunctive form 'do not seek' used after تا to express the intended prohibitive/result. به نزدیک تو is a locative phrase 'near you' (به + نزدیک + pronoun تو) and آب‌روی is the thing sought (your reputation/good name).
segments.s556-g51: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' (indicating source). مکن is the negative imperative 'do not (do)'; it negates the predicate formed by the noun دوستی with the predicative adjective/participle خواستار ('desirous / seeking'), so the line means 'do not be one who seeks friendship from an enemy.'
segments.s556-g52: وگر introduces a concessive 'even if / but if'. چند here functions adverbially ('however many times' / 'repeatedly'), and خواند is a 3rd‑person verb 'calls/summons' with the enclitic ت + را (ترا) together marking 'you' as the object; شهریار is the subject 'the king.'
segments.s556-g53: درختی بود is an existential/past construction 'there was a tree' (درختی + بود). سبز is an adjective 'green'. و بارش is 'and its load/fruit/burden' where ش is the possessive suffix 'its'. کبست is an archaic/obscure noun left untranslated here.
segments.s556-g54: وگر 'if/and if' sets a conditional. پای گیری is a conditional clause where گیری is a present/subjunctive/indefinite action 'you seize/take' with پای as its object ('seize the base/foot'). سر آید is a resultant clause: آید 'comes' (3rd person) with سر 'the head/top' as subject, and به دست 'into the hand' is the locative result.
segments.s556-g55: اگر ... و‌گر ... pairs two conditional prepositional phrases for contrast/emphasis: 'if in a high place and if in a low place' (در + فرازی / در + نشیب). This construction sets up 'wherever/whichever position you are in.'
segments.s556-g56: نباید is an impersonal modal 'one should not / it ought not to be' followed by the infinitive نهادن 'to put/place' (infinitive as complement). سر is the object of نهادن ('one's head'), اندر is the preposition 'into/within', and فریب is the noun 'deception' — the idiom means 'one ought not to place one's head into deceit' (i.e. be deceived).
segments.s556-g9: خم آورد is a compact verbal phrase: خم (bend/curve) + آورد (past of آوردن, 'bring/produce') yields 'bent' or 'caused to bend.' بالای 'atop/above' precedes the noun سرو 'cypress' (a common poetic image). سهی is a poetic adjective 'dark/black' modifying سرو. Word order is typical Persian: locative/phrase after the verb yields 'bent above the dark cypress.'
segments.s556-g10: گل سرخ را is a definite marked noun phrase ('the red flower/rose' with را marking definiteness/object). داد is past tense 'gave' (transitive), and رنگ بهی is a noun phrase 'a fair/beautiful color' serving as the thing given or imparted. The subject is omitted and is carried over from the previous clause, so literally '...gave the red rose a fair color.'
segments.s556-g11: چو ... آمد is a temporal clause: چو 'when' plus آمد 'comes' (simple past/present-habitual form used for 'arrive'). تو is the pronoun 'your/you' modifying روز 'day' → 'when your day comes.' جهاندار باش is an imperative: جهاندار 'sovereign' used predicatively and باش 'be' is the second-person singular imperative, so 'be a sovereign.'
segments.s556-g12: This is a string of coordinated imperatives. خردمند باش = 'be wise' (خردمند adjective + باش imperative). و 'and' links the clauses. بی‌آزار باش = 'be harmless/inoffensive' (بی- negating prefix + آزار 'harm/annoyance'), again with باش. Repetition of باش after each predicate is emphatic and normal in Persian exhortation.
segments.s556-g13: نگر is an imperative 'look' or 'take heed.' تا here introduces a cautionary clause ('so that' / 'lest' in this context) followed by the negative subjunctive/imperfect form نپیچی (ن + پیچی) = 'do not turn.' سر 'head' is literal or idiomatic for 'attention/face'; از means 'from/away from'; دادخواه 'seeker of justice' is the object of that avoidance. Together: 'Be careful that you do not turn your head away from the petitioner for justice.'
segments.s556-g14: نبخشی is negative present/subjunctive or negative imperative of بخشیدن 'to forgive/give' (addressed to 'you'): 'do not forgive.' ستمکارگان را uses را to mark the definite object 'the oppressors.' گناه 'sin' names what should not be forgiven; Persian often places the thing forgiven after the object (literally: 'do not forgive the oppressors (their) sin').
segments.s556-g15: زبان را (language/speech marked by را) is the direct object/topic 'your tongue/speech.' مگردان is a negative imperative 'do not turn' (م- negative prefix + گردان 'turn/turn into'); به گرد means 'toward/about/into the company of' and دروغ 'lies/falsehood.' Overall: 'Do not turn your tongue toward lies' (i.e., do not speak falsehood).
segments.s556-g16: چو here functions as a conditional 'if' (classical usage), خواهی 'you wish/want' and که introduces the subordinate clause. تاج 'crown' + از تو 'from you' = 'the crown from you' and گیرد (third-person singular present/subjunctive form of گرفتن) 'takes/receives.' فروغ 'radiance' is the thing received. The clause conditionally says: 'if you want the crown to take its radiance from you' (i.e., for the crown's splendor to come from your integrity), avoid lying.
segments.s556-g41: سر بردباران is a genitive phrase literally 'the head/of the patient(s)'; here it is used idiomatically to mark what 'befits' or suits a patient person. نیاید is an older/poetic negative present/subjunctive meaning 'should not come' or 'is not fitting'. به + خشم = 'into/with anger' (به expresses movement/tendency into the state named by خشم).
segments.s556-g42: ز نابودنیها 'from/on account of perishable things' (ز = 'from/because of'). بخوابند is a poetic/archaic verbal form meaning 'sleep/slumber' (here an intransitive present-like form placed before its noun). چشم follows as the (logical) subject/object of the verbal phrase: 'the eye slumbers' / 'let not the eye slumber.'
segments.s556-g43: وگر is a conditional 'if / but if'. بردباری is the abstract noun 'patience'. ز حد بگذرد combines the preposition ز 'from' and حد 'limit' with بگذرد (3rd‑person verbal form) meaning 'passes/exceeds the limit' — here an adversative conditional clause.
segments.s556-g44: دلاور is the actor 'the valiant/hero'. گمانی is a noun 'a supposition/feeling' that functions as the direct object of برد ('took' / 'regarded') in this older diction. به سستی is a prepositional phrase 'as/to weakness' (literally 'towards weakness'). برد here is an older verbal use 'took/considered' (sense not listed in the provided options).
segments.s556-g45: هرانکس که باشد is a standard conditional/relative 'whoever is / whoever may be'. باشد is the subjunctive/present form of 'to be' used here in indirect/optative sense. خداوند گاه = 'lord/master for a time' (گاه 'time, occasionally'), so the whole segment introduces 'whomever, when he is master for a while...'.
segments.s556-g46: می‌انجی is an archaic/poetic form with a habitual/occasional prefix می-; the clause functions 'God sometimes ... کند' where کند is the main verb 'does/makes'. خرد را is the definite object marker construction ('wisdom' + را). بر دو راه is a prepositional phrase 'upon/at two roads' i.e. 'at a crossroads' — the verb (کند) places or sets wisdom there.
segments.s556-g47: نه ... نه is the correlative 'neither ... nor'. سستی 'laxity/weakness' and تیزی 'sharpness/harshness' are the two contrasting qualities. به + کاراندرون places these qualities with respect to 'کار اندرون' = 'affairs of the inner/domestic sphere' (i.e. household or internal administration).
segments.s556-g48: خرد باد uses باد as an optative particle 'may it be' (a wish). جان ترا is a vocative/possessive address 'O your life / O my soul' composed of جان + the 2nd‑person suffix ت; the following را here functions as an emphatic/definite marker on the phrase. رهنمون is the predicate noun 'guide/leading' — 'May wisdom be the guide of your soul.'
segments.s556-g65: نگر is an imperative 'look/see/consider' used as an admonition. تا + present-subjunctive نسازی here gives a precautionary meaning 'lest / so that you do not...', and ز introduces the source/basis 'from'. بازوی گنج is a genitive phrase 'the arm/strength of treasure' (بازوی = strength/power, گنج = wealth/treasure) functioning as the object/source of نسازی ('make/rely on').
segments.s556-g66: که is a subordinating/relative particle 'that/so that'; بر تو is a prepositional phrase 'upon you' placed before the verb; سرآید is a present-subjunctive/optative-like form 'come/befall' meaning 'happen to'. سرای سپنج is a noun phrase ('سرای' + 'سپنج') meaning 'sponge-house' (سپنج = 'sponge'); note the dictionary senses given for سرای do not match the noun 'house' here.
segments.s556-g67: مزن is an imperative in an admonitory negative sense 'do not (do)'; رای is the object 'counsel/plan'. جز با = 'except with', so خردمند مرد is the excluded companion 'a wise man' (خردمند modifies مرد). The whole clause means 'take counsel only with a wise man.'
segments.s556-g68: از = 'from/of' introduces separation; آیین شاهان is a genitive construction 'the custom/practice of kings'. پیشی مگرد is an archaic verbal phrase: پیشی = 'precedence' or 'presuming to go before', and مگرد here functions as a prohibitive 'do not become/presume' (the supplied dictionary senses for مگرد do not reflect this verbal use, so -1 is given). The clause warns 'do not presume to outrun the kings' custom.'
segments.s556-g69: بترسان is an imperative 'frighten/terrify'; به + لشکر is instrumental/agentive here 'by/with an army' (use of به to indicate means), and بداندیش را with را marks the definite object 'the ill-minded/malicious one'. The clause orders: 'use the army to intimidate the malicious.'
segments.s556-g70: نگه کن is an imperative 'keep watch' (نگه = watch/keeping, کن = do!), modified by the adverbial phrase به ژرفی 'with depth / deeply' (ژرفی = depth/profundity). پس و پیش را is the object marked by را meaning 'both rear and front' (پس = back/after, پیش = front/before). The whole clause: 'keep a deep watch on both rear and front.'
segments.s556-g71: ستاینده‌ای is a nominal agent 'a praiser/flatterer' (the -ای gives an indefinite/agentive feel). کو is a classical relative particle 'who/that' (the dictionary senses listed do not show this function, so -1). ز بهر هوا = 'for the sake of whim/desire' (ز بهر = 'for/for the sake of', هوا = desire/whim). The clause describes the motive of the praiser.
segments.s556-g72: ستاید is a present/future third-person verb 'praises' (here describing habitual or typical action). کسی را is the definite object 'someone' (را marks the object). همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle 'ever/always', and ناسزا is the manner/content 'insult/abuse' — so the clause says that such a flatterer will 'praise someone with insults.'
segments.s556-g121: تو‌گر is a conditional 'if you' (archaic compound). باهشی here means 'with him/alongside him' (a prepositional/relational element following the condition). مشمر is a participial/imperative form meaning 'girded/armed' (poetic; describes the state of going). او را uses را as the definite object marker attached to او. به دوست is a prepositional phrase 'to/for a friend' and completes the verb phrase implicitly supplied by the clause (i.e. 'present/make [him] to a friend' or 'go with him to a friend').
segments.s556-g122: کجا is the interrogative 'where'. دست is the noun 'hand' (the agent/subject of reaching). یابد is the classical 3rd-person form 'attain/reach' (here rhetorical: 'can/shall reach'). بدردت is بدرد + enclitic ت meaning 'to/for you' and here uses the sense 'cause pain/harm to'; پوست is 'skin' and is the object of that harm. Word order is interrogative: 'Where might the hand reach to cause injury to your skin?'.
segments.s556-g123: شب اورمزد آمد is a simple nominal phrase + verb: 'the night of Ohrmazd came/arrived' (اورمزد = Ahura Mazda). و is the coordinating 'and'. ماه دی is a nominative phrase 'the moon of Dey' (here Dey names the time/month; in context it completes the temporal scene).
segments.s556-g124: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' (start of a phrase meaning 'from speaking'). گفتن is the verbal noun 'speaking/talking'. بیاسای is an imperative/optative 'be at rest/cease' (stop speaking). و 'and' links the imperative to بردار which is an imperative 'take' (here addressed to someone: 'take the ...'). می is the classical word for 'wine'. The clause therefore issues two commands: 'be done with speaking and take the wine.'
segments.s556-g125: کنون is the temporal adverb 'now'. کار دیهیم is a noun + adjective phrase meaning 'the work/affairs of the countryside/people' (دیهیم = rural/peasant, postposed adjective). بهرام ساز is imperative-like: بهرام is the proper name and ساز here functions poetically as 'make/establish' (though the dictionary senses list treats ساز as a noun, in this line it is used verbally/imperatively 'set up / make Bahram'). The whole segment issues the instruction to arrange affairs and set Bahram up.
segments.s556-g126: که introduces the purpose/result clause 'so that / in order that'. در پادشاهی is the locative phrase 'in the kingship/kingdom'. نماند is the negative jussive/optative sense 'may not remain / does not remain' (classical poetic use to express lack of lasting). دراز is the adverb 'long' modifying نماند: 'so that he does not remain long on the throne.'
segments.s556-g33: همه is an adverbial intensifier here meaning 'completely/altogether' modifying the following noun phrase; بردباری is a noun 'patience/forbearance' that is being commanded by the imperative کن ('do' = 'be/show'); و links this command to the next noun (راستی), so the imperative is understood to govern both 'بردباری' and 'راستی'.
segments.s556-g34: جدا + کن functions as an imperative 'separate/remove' (جدا as adverb/adjective 'apart' with the verb کن). ز is the preposition 'from', followed by دل 'heart' which governs the nouns کژی و کاستی ('crookedness and deficiency'); note the poetic word order places the prepositional phrase before the objects and omits the ezâfe linking that would appear in prose.
segments.s556-g35: بپرهیز is an imperative 'refrain/avoid'. تا introduces a purpose/result clause ('so that' / 'lest') and governs the following negative/result clause: بد نگرددت نام is a poetic word order for 'نامِت بد نگردد' — نگردد is the (negative) subjunctive/optative of گردیدن 'to become', with the enclitic -ت marking 'your' possession shifted onto the verb for metrical/poetic reasons; نام is the noun 'name/reputation'.
segments.s556-g36: که introduces an explanatory clause 'for/that'. بدنام is an adjective 'notorious/disgraced'. گیتی 'the world' is the logical subject of نبیند here; نبیند (present/subjunctive/impersonal) literally 'does not see' is idiomatic with به کام 'to/for satisfaction' — together meaning 'the world will not grant satisfaction' (i.e., the notorious will not find the world giving them what they desire).
segments.s556-g37: ز راه خرد is a prepositional phrase 'from the path/way of wisdom'; ایچ گونه is an emphatic adverbial 'in no such way / by no means'; متاب is an imperative-like verb meaning 'do not be agitated/turn (away)' in this context — together the phrase warns against departing from wisdom's way (poetically expressed by the order and emphatic adverb).
segments.s556-g38: پشیمانی 'regret' is the thing brought (object) by آرد 'brings/causes'. دلت را is 'your heart' with the possessive suffix -ت ('your') attached to دل and the object marker را following; the verb آرد precedes the logical subject شتاب 'haste', so the line uses poetic inversion: 'haste brings regret to your heart.'
segments.s556-g39: درنگ 'a pause/delay' is the subject; آورد (causative sense) is 'brings about' or 'causes'; راستیها is the plural 'truths' (object); پدید 'forth/into view' (here an adverbial/verb element meaning 'appear' or 'manifest') — together: 'Delay causes truths to appear' (i.e., pausing brings out what is true).
segments.s556-g40: ز راه خرد is again 'from the path/way of wisdom' (prepositional source). نباید is the impersonal modal 'one should not / it is not right to'; سر کشید is an idiomatic compound where سر ('head') is the element acted on and کشید (infinitive) = 'to draw/raise/turn' — here 'سر نباید کشید ز راه خرد' means 'one must not raise/turn the head away from (i.e. stray from) the way of wisdom.' Note the poetic word order (subject/verb elements shifted).

sentences.s556-s57: The Lord of the world sent a cold wind; then that ruby of the cheeks turned pale.
sentences.s556-s58: When the colorful-faced merchant's countenance darkened, Bahram's heart was stunned by that pain.
sentences.s556-s59: For forty days he lay ill — a pitiable archer and dishonoured; the high throne stood covered with dust and unused.
sentences.s556-s60: Thus it was, so long as the revolving heavens endured: sometimes full of sorrow and sometimes full of love.
sentences.s556-s9: May your spirit be wise and modesty be your rule; speak well and with a gentle voice.
sentences.s556-s10: May the victorious Lord be your ally; may the hearts of your subjects be your conquest.
sentences.s556-s11: Cast away hatred and be far from desire; lest desire become ruler over you.
sentences.s556-s12: Let not a tattletale, an ignorant man, or a pretender find passage before you.
sentences.s556-s53: Give to the arzanians whatever you wish, for your treasure is fitting for the arzanians.
sentences.s556-s54: Spend/sacrifice life and heart as far as you can for (the sake of) jealousy,
sentences.s556-s55: Whenever envy seizes the king, the pious people reproach him.
sentences.s556-s56: When Farrokh the scribe wrote the counsel, he brought it and set it before the vizier.
sentences.s556-s1: When he realized that one cannot escape death, he shed much bloody water from his eyes.
sentences.s556-s2: He spread a carpet in his iwan; he commanded Bahram to come forward.
sentences.s556-s3: He said to him, 'O noble-born son, you have excelled in manliness and learning.'
sentences.s556-s4: Kingship has been placed upon me by nature; the color of my cheek has made my face the same color as my hair.
sentences.s556-s29: Do not make evil thought the centre of your heart; for the ill‑minded, the times were bad.
sentences.s556-s30: Where has the spahbed become an oath‑breaker? Would the famed assembly laugh at him?
sentences.s556-s31: O prudent one, whose ornament is the soul — preserve your speech and your covenant.
sentences.s556-s32: Even the display of crown, treasure, and army represents the turning of sun and moon.
sentences.s556-s49: May your life become keener than the enemy's; may your heart, intellect, and banner be more world‑conquering.
sentences.s556-s50: Find out where desire rose before anyone; regard it so that his judgement does not seize the melody.
sentences.s556-s51: If a friend finds you fresh‑faced, let this name gain colour and scent.
sentences.s556-s52: When you are with your enemy, keep your face full of clang; give the malcontent a colourless countenance.
sentences.s556-s45: Make your tongue and wisdom agree with your heart; drive them thus so that you speak as you wish.
sentences.s556-s46: Whoever has brain in his head — all his counsel and speech are apt (pithy/witty).
sentences.s556-s47: Whenever you are with a counselor, let your words be plain, without (a) assembly.
sentences.s556-s48: If your counsel is seasoned by experience, all your days will be in increase (prosperity).
sentences.s556-s41: Its increase grows greater every day; haste comes, and the heart becomes filled with blood.
sentences.s556-s42: Whoever would fight the sea with its water is not a wise man.
sentences.s556-s43: Make the heart a bow and your tongue an arrow; do not take these my words lightly.
sentences.s556-s44: If the shot is wide, and (so) the right hand — set a mark; that mark is (a sign of) whim/desire.
sentences.s556-s37: That speech constantly seeks your defeat; do not oppose it until it grows old before you.
sentences.s556-s38: If anyone whose praise might serve you comes to your affairs, do not make him merely one of the world's common people.
sentences.s556-s39: For God does not desire praise; he will indeed abase the heart of the one who is blamed.
sentences.s556-s40: Whoever, upon a sinner, shuts his eye and thereby easily swallows his anger.
sentences.s556-s13: From the ignorant you will find nothing but worse things; do not turn your gaze toward the unlearned, do not look at them.
sentences.s556-s14: Consider (a man) such that the shameless and the garrulous have no reputation before anyone.
sentences.s556-s15: Make wisdom your moon and keep anger as a servant; do not be harsh with a pious man.
sentences.s556-s16: Be watchful so that greed does not come upon you, for greed brings anger, fear, and need.
sentences.s556-s25: Keep watch so that fault-finding people do not seek to harm your good name nearby.
sentences.s556-s26: Do not seek friendship with an enemy, even if the king calls you repeatedly.
sentences.s556-s27: There was a green tree bearing 'kebast'; but if you seize its base, the top will come into your hand.
sentences.s556-s28: Whether on a height or in a low place, one should not put one's head into deception.
sentences.s556-s5: She bent above the dark cypress; the red rose took on a lovely color.
sentences.s556-s6: When your day comes, be a sovereign; be wise and be harmless.
sentences.s556-s7: Take heed: do not turn your head away from one who seeks justice; do not forgive the oppressors their sin.
sentences.s556-s8: Do not turn your tongue to lies, if you wish the crown to take its radiance from you.
sentences.s556-s21: It does not become the patient to be angry; let not the eye slumber over things that will perish.
sentences.s556-s22: But if patience exceeds its limit, the valiant will take that as a sign of weakness.
sentences.s556-s23: Whoever is master for a time, God sometimes sets wisdom at a crossroads.
sentences.s556-s24: Neither laxity nor sharpness in domestic affairs; may wisdom be, O soul, your guide.
sentences.s556-s33: Beware lest you build on the strength of treasure, for the sponge-house may come upon you.
sentences.s556-s34: Form no counsel except with a wise man; do not depart from the customs of kings.
sentences.s556-s35: Frighten the ill-minded with (your) army; keep a deep watch on both rear and front.
sentences.s556-s36: A flatterer who praises for whim will praise someone with insults.
sentences.s556-s61: If you go with him (as a companion) armed and present him to a friend, where could his hand reach to wound your skin?
sentences.s556-s62: The night of Ohrmazd came and the moon of Dey — cease from speaking and take the wine.
sentences.s556-s63: Now set the rural affairs in order; make Bahram (king), so that he does not remain long in the kingship.
sentences.s556-s17: Be wholly patient and be upright; remove crookedness and lack from your heart.
sentences.s556-s18: Take care so that your name does not become bad, for the notorious find no satisfaction in the world.
sentences.s556-s19: Do not, in any way, turn away from the path of wisdom; haste will bring regret to your heart.
sentences.s556-s20: Delay reveals truths; one must not lift one's head from the path of wisdom.
